title: FIFA’s Boss Gets a Big Boost for a Fourth Term — Wait, Is That Even Allowed?
summary: Imagine you’re the boss of world soccer. You’ve already been in charge for three terms. Now you want a fourth. But the rules say you can only have three. What do you do? Gianni Infantino, the president of FIFA (the group that runs soccer worldwide), says his first term doesn’t really count. He took over in 2016 after the last boss, Sepp Blatter, got into big trouble for corruption (dishonest or illegal behavior). So Infantino says he can run again in 2027. And now he has a huge advantage. On Thursday, two big groups of countries — Africa and Asia — said they will support him. Together, they have 101 votes out of 211. That’s almost half! South America already said yes too, adding 10 more votes. Infantino has done some things people like, such as giving more money to soccer groups around the world — $2.7 billion from 2027 to 2030, which is eight times more than 10 years ago. But he’s also done things people argue about. He made the World Cup bigger, with 48 teams instead of 32. He started a new 32-team club tournament. And he gave a peace prize to former U.S. President Donald Trump, which some people said was unfair because FIFA is supposed to stay neutral (not pick sides). Still, with so many votes already lined up, Infantino looks hard to beat. Will anyone else even try to run against him?
why_it_matters: The way a leader can challenge rules to stay in power teaches you how even big sports organizations have politics, just like your school club elections.

keywords.explanation: The organization that runs soccer all over the world and organizes the World Cup.
Dishonest or illegal behavior, often for personal gain.
Not taking sides or showing favoritism in an argument or competition.
A series of games or matches that teams or people play to find a winner.
Choices made by members of a group to decide something, like who becomes a leader.
An award for someone who has worked for peace, though giving it to a former president caused disagreement.

questions.question: What is Gianni Infantino trying to do in the article?
options: Run for a fourth term as FIFA president
questions.correct_answer: Run for a fourth term as FIFA president

questions.question: Why does Infantino say his first term should not count?
options: He took over after Sepp Blatter was banned for corruption
He won fewer votes than others
The World Cup was canceled that year
questions.correct_answer: He took over after Sepp Blatter was banned for corruption

questions.question: How many votes do Africa and Asia have together?
What did Infantino give to former U.S. President Donald Trump?
How many teams does the expanded World Cup now have?
What does it mean for FIFA to stay neutral?
options: To not pick sides in political or other conflicts
To give money to every country equally
questions.correct_answer: To not pick sides in political or other conflicts

background_read: FIFA, founded in 1904, runs the World Cup and has 211 member nations that vote for its president.
Sepp Blatter (the previous president) was banned for corruption in 2015, which led Gianni Infantino to take over.
FIFA has often been criticized for scandals and decisions about where the World Cup is held.
Article_Structure: WHO: Gianni Infantino, the president of FIFA, and the voting member countries.
WHAT: Infantino is seeking a fourth term even though the rules normally allow only three, and he already has support from over half the voters.
WHERE: In FIFA, the worldwide governing body for soccer.
WHY: He says his first term was not a full term and he has increased money for soccer groups, but critics argue he is ignoring limits and making controversial choices.

perspectives.description: They say Infantino has increased money for smaller soccer countries and expanded the World Cup to include more teams, giving more nations a chance.
They argue he is ignoring term limits, making decisions that benefit his allies, and awarding a peace prize to a controversial political figure, which hurts FIFA's neutrality.
They point out that with so many guaranteed votes, the election may not be competitive, which raises questions about democratic process within FIFA.
